pr_title: DOM-45299 add endpoint for UI to call in order to implement hide project copy button

comments.author: niole
comments.body: toying around with the new "best practice" for apis: https://docs.google.com/document/d/18UQ242kR5Cb-_RaSElnmIQui4pu6seh7YqPlxWG5snA/edit#heading=h.nscqknnl8lti. Should I make another routes file that doesn't include the `/v4/projects` at the beginning of the url?

comments.author: ddl-grequeni
comments.body:
_Apologies for the wall of text..._ 🐱 

The UI is already using `v4/projects/permissions/all` to get the `canCopy` and `canFork` attributes. Should we integrate the two solutions? Would this new logic apply to fork operation too? I imagine one possible option is to have a new subresource of project `v4/projects/:projectId/permissions` and have all the logic of "canCopy" in one place. Maybe `permissions` doesn't make sense for the current logic, so something more generic could work better, like `v4/projects/:projectId/actions` and then have a list of actions 
```
[{id: "copy", supported: true, reason: null, uri: "http://..../v4/projects/:projectId/copy"},
 {id: "fork", supported: false, reason: "...", uri: "..."}]
``` 
I think some approach like this would give a more "RESTful" navigation to the API too (collection/id/collection).

About the versions, I would avoid mixing v1 and v4 in the same path.
`v4/projects` is the internal, legacy, "non-standard" version of the API. Then we have `projects/v1/projects` which is the "public", closer to the standard one. We could start a new API even closer to the standard using `/{domain}/v1/projects/:projectId/actions` format (only defining that single endpoint for now). The {domain} could be "develop" as a more coarse grained product domain, encapsulating projects, environments, workspaces, etc. IMO for this particular ticket, we should be fine with just defining a new endpoint under the legacy v4, or creating a brand new API `/develop/v1` with only this new endpoint.

If we do a new API, I would go with the spec first approach (like public API) rather than resty. It's easy to make mistakes in resty definitions, that go unseen in code reviews and are only detected when we do a system test. The spec first approach means we manually define the yaml we are going to give to customers, so the code review is easier (not having to guess what the auto-generated swagger is going to look like from resty files and scala code). And the implementation must use the generated code, so there is an extra layer of validation that the spec is correct.
